DPDP Section Title: Ground for Processing of Personal Data
Requirement Description: Processing of personal data must be based on consent of the Data Principal or for certain legitimate uses specified in Section 7
Plain English Explanation: You must have a valid legal basis to process personal data. The primary basis is consent. Alternative bases include government functions, employment, medical emergencies, and disasters.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Terms & Conditions, Consent Form
Keywords for Detection: consent, legal basis, lawful basis, ground for processing, legitimate use, process personal data, basis for processing
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy clause, Consent mechanism records, Legal basis assessment document
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy clearly specify the legal basis for processing personal data?
Sample Compliant Language: We process your personal data based on your explicit consent, which you provide when you [sign up/use our services]. In certain cases, we may process personal data for legitimate uses such as [employment/medical emergencies/legal compliance] as permitted under Section 7 of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
Remediation Recommendation: Add a dedicated 'Legal Basis for Processing' clause that explicitly identifies the basis (consent or legitimate use) for each category of data processing activity.
Notes: This is the foundational requirement. Without a valid legal basis, all processing is unlawful.

DPDP Section Title: Consent Must Be Free, Specific, Informed, Unconditional, and Unambiguous
Requirement Description: Consent must be free, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous through clear affirmative action. Pre-ticked boxes or implied consent are invalid.
Plain English Explanation: Users must actively and clearly agree to data processing through a positive action. Silence, pre-ticked boxes, or inactivity cannot be treated as consent.
Applicable Document: Consent Form, Privacy Policy, Cookie Notice, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: consent, freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous, affirmative action, clear action, opt-in, explicit consent, pre-ticked, implied consent
Evidence Required: Consent form UI screenshots, Click-through records, Audit trail of consent timestamps
Compliance Test Question: Is consent obtained through a clear affirmative action without pre-ticked boxes or implied consent mechanisms?
Sample Compliant Language: By clicking the 'I Agree' button below, you expressly consent to the collection, use, storage, and processing of your personal data as described in this Privacy Policy. You acknowledge that this consent is freely given, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous.
Remediation Recommendation: Replace any pre-ticked boxes, toggle defaults, or implied consent with explicit opt-in mechanisms requiring affirmative user action.
Notes: Article 7 of DPDP mirrors GDPR consent standards. This is the most litigated area.

DPDP Section Title: Consent Must Be Requested Separately for Each Purpose
Requirement Description: If processing is for multiple purposes, consent must be sought separately for each purpose. Granular consent is required.
Plain English Explanation: If you use data for multiple reasons (e.g., service delivery AND marketing), users must be able to consent to each purpose separately.
Applicable Document: Consent Form, Privacy Policy, Cookie Settings
Keywords for Detection: separate consent, granular consent, purpose-specific, individual purpose, each purpose, separate permission, granular choice
Evidence Required: Consent management platform records showing granular consent choices, Purpose-specific consent logs
Compliance Test Question: Does the consent mechanism allow users to provide separate consent for each distinct processing purpose?
Sample Compliant Language: You may provide consent for each purpose separately: (a) Consent for Service Delivery [YES/NO]; (b) Consent for Marketing Communications [YES/NO]; (c) Consent for Analytics and Improvement [YES/NO]. You may accept or decline each purpose independently.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement a consent management interface that displays each processing purpose distinctly with separate opt-in toggles.
Notes: Bundled consent is prohibited. Each purpose must be independently consentable.

DPDP Section Title: Right to Access and Manage Consent
Requirement Description: Data Principal must be able to access and manage their consent through an accessible, transparent, and interoperable platform
Plain English Explanation: Users must have a way to view what they have consented to and manage/update those choices easily.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, User Dashboard, Account Settings
Keywords for Detection: access consent, manage consent, consent dashboard, consent preferences, consent settings, view consent, consent portal, consent manager
Evidence Required: Consent dashboard UI, User access logs to consent management, Interoperability documentation
Compliance Test Question: Can Data Principals access a dashboard or interface to view and manage their consent preferences?
Sample Compliant Language: You may access and manage your consent preferences at any time by visiting your [Account Settings > Privacy Preferences] or by contacting our Grievance Officer. Your consent choices are stored in an accessible and interoperable format.
Remediation Recommendation: Build or integrate a consent management platform where users can view, modify, and withdraw consent for each purpose.
Notes: This is a new DPDP-specific requirement not explicitly found in GDPR.

Requirement Description: Consent must be obtained before or at the time of collection of personal data, unless processing is for legitimate uses under Section 7
Plain English Explanation: You cannot collect data first and ask for consent later. Consent must come before or simultaneously with collection.
Applicable Document: Consent Form, Registration Flow, Onboarding Process
Keywords for Detection: before processing, prior consent, consent before collection, obtain consent first, pre-collection consent, advance consent
Evidence Required: Onboarding flow audit showing consent before data entry, Timestamp comparison: consent timestamp precedes data collection timestamp
Compliance Test Question: Is consent obtained before or at the same time as personal data collection, not after?
Sample Compliant Language: We obtain your consent before collecting any personal data. You will be prompted to provide consent prior to entering your personal information in our registration forms.
Remediation Recommendation: Restructure data collection flows so that no data is collected before consent is obtained. Implement technical controls to block data submission until consent is confirmed.
Notes: Retroactive consent is invalid. Technical controls must enforce consent-before-collection.

Requirement Description: Data Principal has the right to withdraw consent at any time with the ease of withdrawal being comparable to the ease of giving consent. Upon withdrawal, processing must cease and data must be deleted unless retention is required by law.
Plain English Explanation: Users must be able to withdraw consent as easily as they gave it. After withdrawal, you must stop processing their data and delete it unless a law requires you to keep it.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Terms & Conditions, Account Settings, Consent Dashboard
Keywords for Detection: withdraw consent, revoke consent, consent withdrawal, opt-out, withdraw permission, withdraw at any time, cease processing, delete upon withdrawal, as easy to withdraw
Evidence Required: Withdrawal mechanism UI, Time-to-withdraw vs time-to-consent metrics, Deletion confirmation records, Processing cessation logs
Compliance Test Question: Can Data Principals withdraw consent as easily as they gave it, and is data deleted upon withdrawal unless legally required to retain?
Sample Compliant Language: You have the right to withdraw your consent at any time by clicking the 'Withdraw Consent' button in your Privacy Settings or by emailing our Grievance Officer at [email]. Withdrawing consent is as easy as giving it. Upon withdrawal, we will cease processing your personal data and delete it within [X days], unless retention is required by applicable law.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement a one-click consent withdrawal mechanism that is as prominent and accessible as the consent mechanism. Automate data deletion workflows triggered by withdrawal.
Notes: The 'ease of withdrawal' requirement is subjective but critical. Best practice: one-click withdrawal.

DPDP Section Title: Notice at the Time of Consent
Requirement Description: At the time of seeking consent, the Data Fiduciary must provide a notice containing specified information about identity, purpose, data recipients, grievance mechanism, and rights
Plain English Explanation: When asking for consent, you must simultaneously provide a clear notice telling the user who you are, why you need the data, who else will see it, how to complain, and what their rights are.
Applicable Document: Consent Notice, Privacy Policy Summary, Pop-up Notice, Registration Form
Keywords for Detection: notice, consent notice, at the time of consent, notice accompanying consent, information provided with consent, notice requirements
Evidence Required: Consent notice copy, Notice delivery mechanism, Notice receipt acknowledgment
Compliance Test Question: Is a comprehensive notice provided to the Data Principal at the exact time consent is sought?
Sample Compliant Language: Before you provide consent, please read this notice: [Company Name], located at [Address], will process your personal data for [specific purposes]. Your data may be shared with [categories of recipients]. You have the right to access, correct, and erase your data. To raise concerns, contact our Grievance Officer at [email]. For full details, see our Privacy Policy [link].
Remediation Recommendation: Attach a concise notice alongside every consent request that covers all Section 5(6) requirements. Do not rely solely on a link to the full Privacy Policy.
Notes: The notice must be provided 'at the time of seeking consent' - not before or after.

Requirement Description: Upon withdrawal of consent, the Data Fiduciary must ensure that the personal data is erased and no longer processed, unless retention is necessary for a prescribed purpose or legal obligation
Plain English Explanation: When someone withdraws consent, you must delete their data and stop using it. The only exception is if a law specifically requires you to keep it.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Retention Policy, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: consequences of withdrawal, effect of withdrawal, erase upon withdrawal, deletion after withdrawal, stop processing, data deletion on withdrawal
Evidence Required: Automated deletion workflows, Deletion confirmation records, Retention schedule for legally-required data
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization have automated processes to erase data and cease processing upon consent withdrawal?
Sample Compliant Language: Upon your withdrawal of consent, we will: (a) immediately cease all processing of your personal data; (b) initiate erasure of your personal data within [X days]; and (c) notify any Data Processors to delete your data. If any law requires us to retain your data, we will retain only the minimum necessary and inform you of this retention.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement automated workflows that trigger data deletion across all systems when consent is withdrawn, with exception handling for legally-mandated retention.
Notes: Must be read with Section 12 (erasure right). Both withdrawal and erasure requests trigger deletion obligations.

DPDP Section Title: Consent Validity for Processing by Consent Manager
Requirement Description: Where a Consent Manager is used, the consent given through the Consent Manager is deemed to be consent given to the Data Fiduciary
Plain English Explanation: If you use a third-party consent management tool (like a CMP), consent collected through that tool is legally valid as if collected directly by you.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Terms & Conditions, Consent Manager Agreement
Keywords for Detection: consent manager, CMP, consent management platform, third party consent, deemed consent, consent intermediary
Evidence Required: Consent Manager agreement, Consent Manager registration certificate, Data processing agreement with Consent Manager
Compliance Test Question: If a Consent Manager is used, is there a disclosure in the Privacy Policy and a valid agreement with the Consent Manager?
Sample Compliant Language: We use [Consent Manager Name], a registered Consent Manager under the DPDP Act, to manage your consent preferences. Consent provided through [Consent Manager Name] is treated as consent provided directly to us. The Consent Manager acts as your agent and does not process your personal data for its own purposes.
Remediation Recommendation: If using a CMP, register it if required, ensure it complies with DPDP requirements, and disclose its use in the Privacy Policy.
Notes: Consent Managers must be registered with the Data Protection Board as per the Act.

Requirement Description: Processing of personal data of a child (person below 18 years) requires verifiable consent of the parent or lawful guardian. The consent mechanism must be verifiable.
Plain English Explanation: If you collect data from anyone under 18, you need verifiable permission from their parent or legal guardian. You must have a way to verify they are actually the parent/guardian.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Age Gate, Parental Consent Flow, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: child, children, minor, under 18, below 18, parental consent, guardian consent, verifiable parental consent, age verification, parent permission
Evidence Required: Age verification mechanism, Parental consent flow, Verification method documentation, Age gate implementation
Compliance Test Question: Does the platform obtain verifiable parental consent before processing personal data of users below 18 years of age?
Sample Compliant Language: We do not knowingly collect personal data from children under 18 years of age without verifiable consent from a parent or lawful guardian. If you are under 18, please do not provide any personal data without parental involvement. We use [method] to verify parental consent. If we discover that we have collected data from a child without valid parental consent, we will delete such data immediately.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement an age-gate at entry point, parental consent workflow with verification (email/SMS/ID verification), and automated deletion if parental consent is not obtained within a specified timeframe.
Notes: The age threshold in India is 18 (unlike GDPR's 16 with Member State variation to 13). No relaxation is permitted.

DPDP Section Title: Prohibition on Tracking, Behavioural Monitoring, and Targeted Advertising for Children
Requirement Description: Data Fiduciaries are prohibited from tracking, behavioral monitoring of children, and targeted advertising directed at children
Plain English Explanation: You cannot track children online, monitor their behavior, or show them targeted ads. Even with parental consent, these activities are prohibited.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Ad Tech Policy, Cookie Policy, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: tracking, behavioral monitoring, targeted advertising, directed at children, child tracking, behavioral advertising children, monitoring children, profiling children
Evidence Required: Ad tech configuration showing no behavioral tracking for minors, Cookie configuration audit, Age-based advertising restriction implementation
Compliance Test Question: Is there a technical and policy prohibition on tracking, behavioral monitoring, and targeted advertising directed at children?
Sample Compliant Language: We strictly prohibit: (a) tracking or monitoring the online behavior of children; (b) behavioral profiling of children; and (c) targeted advertising directed at children. If we identify a user as being under 18 years, all tracking cookies and behavioral advertising mechanisms are automatically disabled for that user.
Remediation Recommendation: Configure ad-tech and analytics to exclude users flagged as under-18. Disable behavioral tracking cookies for child users. Implement age-gating that blocks tracking before age is verified.
Notes: This is an absolute prohibition under DPDP - even with parental consent, these activities cannot be conducted for children.

DPDP Section Title: Processing of Children's Data by Government-Notified Classes of Data Fiduciaries
Requirement Description: The Central Government may notify certain classes of Data Fiduciaries who are exempt from verifiable parental consent requirements subject to prescribed conditions
Plain English Explanation: Certain types of organizations (like educational institutions, healthcare providers) may be exempted by the government from strict parental consent requirements, subject to conditions.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Compliance Assessment, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: government notification, notified class, exempted, children's data exemption, class of fiduciaries, prescribed conditions
Evidence Required: Government notification applicable to the entity, Compliance with prescribed conditions
Compliance Test Question: If claiming an exemption for children's data processing, is there a valid government notification applicable to the entity class?
Sample Compliant Language: [If applicable: We fall within the class of Data Fiduciaries notified under [Notification Reference] and are accordingly exempt from the requirement to obtain verifiable parental consent, subject to the following conditions: [list conditions].]
Remediation Recommendation: Monitor government notifications for exemptions. If claiming exemption, maintain documentation of the applicable notification and ensure compliance with all conditions.
Notes: This provision is not yet fully operational as government notifications are still being issued. Monitor for updates.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciaries must not process personal data likely to cause detrimental effect to the well-being of a child, and must make reasonable efforts to verify age and obtain parental consent
Plain English Explanation: You must try to verify if a user is a child and get parental consent before processing data that could harm the child's well-being.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Age Verification System, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: age verification, verify age, age gate, date of birth verification, age confirmation, likely to cause detrimental effect, child well-being
Evidence Required: Age verification mechanism design, Documentation of reasonable efforts, Impact assessment for child-detrimental processing
Compliance Test Question: Are reasonable efforts made to verify the age of users and prevent processing that may harm children's well-being?
Sample Compliant Language: We implement reasonable age verification measures including [describe mechanism - e.g., date of birth declaration, email-based verification]. We do not process personal data in any manner likely to cause detrimental effect to the well-being of a child. If we become aware that we are processing data of a child without parental consent, we will immediately cease such processing and delete the data.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement age-gating mechanism at signup or before data collection. Use DOB collection or third-party age verification. Flag child accounts for restricted processing.
Notes: 'Reasonable efforts' is a flexible standard. Document your approach to demonstrate compliance.

DPDP Section Title: Processing for Government-Provided Services and Benefits
Requirement Description: Personal data may be processed for the performance of any function under any law by the State or for providing any service or benefit to the Data Principal
Plain English Explanation: Government agencies and entities providing government services can process data without consent if it's necessary to provide a government service or benefit to the individual.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Government Service Agreement
Keywords for Detection: government service, state function, benefit to data principal, public service, government benefit, statutory function
Evidence Required: Legal basis assessment showing Section 7(a) applicability, Government authorization documentation
Compliance Test Question: If relying on Section 7(a), is the processing genuinely for a State function or service/benefit to the Data Principal?
Sample Compliant Language: Where we process personal data as part of a government-authorized service or benefit program under [Act/Scheme Name], such processing is based on Section 7(a) of the DPDP Act as a legitimate use. We will inform you of the specific government function or service being performed.
Remediation Recommendation: If relying on Section 7(a), document the specific State function or service being performed and maintain legal basis assessment.
Notes: Primarily applicable to government entities and government service providers.

DPDP Section Title: Processing Under Any Law or Court Order
Requirement Description: Personal data may be processed under any law for the time being in force in India or any judgment or order of any Court or Tribunal
Plain English Explanation: If another Indian law or a court order requires you to process data, you can do so without consent. But you must identify the specific law or order.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Legal Compliance Policy
Keywords for Detection: legal obligation, court order, tribunal order, statutory requirement, legal requirement, compliance with law, judgment of court, legal process
Evidence Required: Legal basis register citing specific law or court order, Documentation of legal obligation
Compliance Test Question: When processing personal data under legal obligation, is the specific law or court order identified and documented?
Sample Compliant Language: We may process your personal data where required to comply with a legal obligation under Indian law, including [specify applicable laws e.g., Income Tax Act, Companies Act], or to comply with a valid order of a court or tribunal. In such cases, consent is not required under Section 7(b) of the DPDP Act.
Remediation Recommendation: Maintain a legal basis register that identifies the specific law or court order for each processing activity relying on Section 7(b).
Notes: Must specify the exact legal provision being relied upon. Vague references to 'legal compliance' are insufficient.

Requirement Description: Personal data may be processed to respond to a medical emergency involving a threat to the life or immediate threat to the health of the Data Principal or any other individual
Plain English Explanation: In a medical emergency where someone's life or health is at immediate risk, you can process their data without consent to provide medical treatment.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Emergency Response Protocol
Keywords for Detection: medical emergency, threat to life, health emergency, immediate threat, medical treatment, healthcare emergency, life-threatening
Evidence Required: Emergency response protocol, Medical emergency processing log (anonymized)
Compliance Test Question: If processing personal data for medical emergencies, is there a documented protocol limiting processing to genuine emergencies?
Sample Compliant Language: In the event of a medical emergency involving an immediate threat to life or health, we may process personal data without consent to the extent necessary to respond to such emergency, as permitted under Section 7(c) of the DPDP Act.
Remediation Recommendation: Establish a clear emergency processing protocol that limits data use to what is strictly necessary for the emergency response.
Notes: Primarily relevant for healthcare providers and emergency services.

Requirement Description: Personal data may be processed for purposes related to employment, including recruitment, termination, and verification, and for providing any service or benefit sought by a Data Principal who is an employee
Plain English Explanation: Employers can process employee data without separate consent for employment-related purposes like hiring, firing, background checks, and providing employee benefits.
Applicable Document: Employee Privacy Policy, HR Policy, Terms of Employment
Keywords for Detection: employment, employee data, recruitment, termination, verification, HR processing, employer, employment purpose, personnel records
Evidence Required: Employee privacy notice, Employment contract data clause, HR processing inventory
Compliance Test Question: If processing employee data under Section 7(d), is the processing limited to employment-related purposes and are employees informed?
Sample Compliant Language: As your employer, we process your personal data for employment-related purposes including recruitment, background verification, payroll administration, performance management, statutory compliance, and provision of employee benefits, under Section 7(d) of the DPDP Act. This processing is necessary for the employment relationship and does not require separate consent.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement an employee privacy notice separate from the customer privacy policy that addresses employment-specific processing.
Notes: Does not give blanket permission for all processing. Must be genuinely employment-related.

DPDP Section Title: Processing for National Security, Sovereignty, and Public Order
Requirement Description: Personal data may be processed in the interest of sovereignty and integrity of India, security of the State, friendly relations with foreign States, maintenance of public order, or preventing incitement to any cognizable offence
Plain English Explanation: Government agencies can process data without consent for national security, maintaining public order, and preventing serious crimes. This is a government-specific exemption.
Applicable Document: Internal Government Policy, Security Protocol
Keywords for Detection: national security, sovereignty, public order, state security, friendly relations, prevention of offense, security of state
Evidence Required: Government authorization, Security clearance documentation
Compliance Test Question: If relying on Section 7(e), is there proper government authorization for the specific processing activity?
Sample Compliant Language: [Applicable to government agencies only] We may process personal data where necessary in the interest of sovereignty and integrity of India, security of the State, maintenance of public order, or prevention of cognizable offences, as authorized under Section 7(e) of the DPDP Act.
Remediation Recommendation: This provision is primarily for government agencies. Private entities should generally not rely on this basis.
Notes: This is a government-specific provision. Private sector entities rarely rely on this basis.

DPDP Section Title: Notice Requirements - Identity and Contact Details
Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must give notice containing: (a) description of personal data collected and purpose of processing; (b) the manner in which Data Principal may exercise their rights
Plain English Explanation: You must tell users what data you collect, why you collect it, and how they can exercise their rights (access, correction, deletion, etc.)
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Notice of Information Practices, Website Footer
Keywords for Detection: personal data collected, types of data, categories of data, data we collect, information collected, collection purpose, purpose of processing, what we collect
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy with data inventory, Notice document, Website notice placement
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy clearly describe the categories of personal data collected and the specific purposes for which it is processed?
Sample Compliant Language: We collect the following categories of personal data: [Identity Data: name, date of birth]; [Contact Data: email, phone, address]; [Technical Data: IP address, browser type, device information]. We collect this data for the following purposes: [Purpose 1: Providing our services]; [Purpose 2: Customer communication]; [Purpose 3: Service improvement].
Remediation Recommendation: Conduct a data mapping exercise and create a data inventory. Publish this information in the Privacy Policy in a clear, structured format.
Notes: Section 8 notice requirements are exhaustive. All sub-sections must be complied with.

Requirement Description: Notice must include the identity and contact details of the Data Fiduciary and the Data Protection Officer or Grievance Officer
Plain English Explanation: You must clearly state who you are (company name, address) and provide contact details for your Data Protection Officer or Grievance Officer.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Contact Page, Website Footer
Suggested Clause Name: Who We Are and How to Contact Us
Keywords for Detection: identity, contact details, data protection officer, DPO, grievance officer, company name, registered address, contact us, who we are
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy with complete identity and contact details, DPO/Grievance Officer appointment records
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy clearly identify the Data Fiduciary and provide contact details of the Grievance Officer?
Sample Compliant Language: Data Fiduciary: [Company Name], a company incorporated in India with CIN [number], having its registered office at [full address]. Grievance Officer: [Name], [Designation], [Email], [Phone], [Address]. Data Protection Officer (if appointed): [Name], [Email], [Phone].
Remediation Recommendation: Include a prominent section in the Privacy Policy with complete identity details. Ensure the Grievance Officer's contact information is accurate and monitored.
Notes: The Grievance Officer must be a real, reachable person. Generic emails like info@company.com do not comply.

Requirement Description: Notice must include description of recipients or categories of recipients to whom personal data may be disclosed
Plain English Explanation: You must tell users who else will receive their data - whether other companies, group entities, service providers, or government agencies.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Sharing Disclosure
Suggested Clause Name: Who We Share Your Data With
Keywords for Detection: share, disclosure, recipients, third parties, categories of recipients, data sharing, disclosed to, transferred to, third party recipients, service providers, business partners
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy with recipient categories, Data sharing agreements inventory, Vendor list with data processing agreements
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy describe the categories of recipients to whom personal data may be disclosed?
Sample Compliant Language: We may share your personal data with the following categories of recipients: (a) Service Providers and Data Processors who assist us in [describe functions]; (b) Group Companies and Affiliates for [purposes]; (c) Government Authorities and Regulatory Bodies when required by law; (d) Professional Advisors such as auditors and legal consultants; and (e) Business Partners with your consent.
Remediation Recommendation: Map all data sharing relationships and categorize recipients. Disclose these categories in the Privacy Policy with brief descriptions of each category.
Notes: Must disclose categories, not necessarily individual entity names unless specifically required.

Requirement Description: Notice must include description of the period for which personal data will be retained or the criteria for determining such period
Plain English Explanation: You must tell users how long you will keep their data, or explain how you decide the retention period if you can't specify an exact timeframe.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Retention Policy
Suggested Clause Name: How Long We Keep Your Data
Keywords for Detection: retention period, retain, storage period, keep data, how long, retention schedule, data retention, stored for, criteria for retention, determine retention
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy with retention periods, Data Retention Schedule, Retention criteria documentation
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy specify the retention period for personal data or the criteria used to determine retention?
Sample Compliant Language: We retain your personal data for the following periods: [Account Data: retained for the duration of your account plus 3 years after closure]; [Transaction Data: retained for 7 years as required by tax laws]; [Marketing Preferences: retained until you withdraw consent or for 2 years of inactivity]. Where no specific period is stated, we use the following criteria to determine retention: [legal obligations, ongoing business relationship, dispute resolution needs].
Remediation Recommendation: Create a data retention schedule that maps each data category to its retention period. Publish this information in the Privacy Policy.
Notes: Specific periods are preferred over vague criteria. 'As long as necessary' is non-compliant.

Requirement Description: Notice must describe the procedure for exercising the right to make a complaint to the Board and the grievance redressal mechanism available to the Data Principal
Plain English Explanation: You must tell users how to file a complaint with your Grievance Officer and how to escalate to the Data Protection Board of India if unresolved.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Grievance Redressal Policy, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: complaint, grievance, grievance officer, grievance mechanism, grievance redressal, redressal mechanism, how to complain, make a complaint, Data Protection Board, escalate complaint
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy with complaint procedure, Grievance mechanism documentation, Board complaint procedure
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy describe both the internal grievance mechanism and the right to complain to the Data Protection Board?
Sample Compliant Language: If you have any concerns about our data processing practices, you may: (1) Contact our Grievance Officer at [email/address] for resolution within [X days]; (2) If unsatisfied with our response, you have the right to file a complaint with the Data Protection Board of India as established under the DPDP Act, 2023. The Board can be contacted at [contact details if available].
Remediation Recommendation: Include a dedicated complaints section in the Privacy Policy with clear steps for internal grievance filing and escalation to the Data Protection Board.
Notes: The Data Protection Board is the appellate authority. Users must be informed of their right to approach it.

Requirement Description: Notice must include description of cross-border transfers of personal data, if any, and the mechanism or country to which data may be transferred
Plain English Explanation: If you transfer data outside India, you must tell users which countries the data goes to and on what basis ( adequacy decision, contract, etc.)
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Cross-Border Transfer Policy
Keywords for Detection: cross-border, international transfer, transfer outside India, overseas transfer, foreign country, data transfer mechanism, adequacy, approved country, data localization
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy with transfer destinations, Cross-border transfer mechanism documentation, Approved country list
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy disclose cross-border data transfers, destination countries, and the legal basis for such transfers?
Sample Compliant Language: Your personal data may be transferred to and processed in countries outside India, including [list countries or categories]. We ensure appropriate safeguards are in place for such transfers, including transfers to countries notified by the Government of India as having adequate data protection or through approved contractual clauses. For details, contact our Grievance Officer.
Remediation Recommendation: Map all cross-border data flows. Disclose destination countries in the Privacy Policy. Ensure transfers comply with Section 16 requirements.
Notes: Government has not yet notified the list of approved countries. Monitor for notifications.

Requirement Description: Notice must be clear, concise, easily accessible, and provided in English or any language specified in the Eighth Schedule to the Constitution
Plain English Explanation: The notice must be easy to understand, not hidden in fine print, and available in English or any scheduled Indian language.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Website Design, Mobile App
Keywords for Detection: clear, concise, easily accessible, plain language, simple language, understandable, accessible format, schedule language, Indian language
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy readability assessment, Accessibility audit, Language availability documentation
Compliance Test Question: Is the Privacy Policy written in clear, concise language that an average user can understand, and is it easily accessible from the homepage?
Sample Compliant Language: This Privacy Policy is written in plain language to ensure you understand how we handle your personal data. It is accessible from every page of our website [footer link] and mobile app [settings > privacy]. We currently provide this notice in English [and Hindi/Marathi/etc.].
Remediation Recommendation: Simplify Privacy Policy language (target Flesch Reading Ease score of 50+). Ensure a Privacy Policy link is visible on every page. Consider providing translations in local languages.
Notes: The Eighth Schedule includes 22 languages. While English is sufficient, providing local language versions enhances compliance.

DPDP Section Title: Notice for Data Not Collected Directly from Data Principal
Requirement Description: Where personal data is not collected directly from the Data Principal, notice must be given within a reasonable period but not later than the first communication or disclosure to another recipient
Plain English Explanation: If you get data about someone from another source (not directly from them), you must tell them about it within a reasonable time - before you first use it or share it.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Indirect Collection Notice, Third-Party Data Receipt Protocol
Keywords for Detection: not collected directly, indirect collection, third party source, received from another source, not from data principal, collected indirectly, sourced from
Evidence Required: Indirect collection notice template, Third-party data intake log, Notice delivery records
Compliance Test Question: When personal data is received from sources other than the Data Principal, is notice provided within a reasonable period before first use or disclosure?
Sample Compliant Language: Where we receive your personal data from sources other than you (e.g., from business partners, public sources, or service providers), we will provide you with the notice required under Section 8 of the DPDP Act within [X days] of receiving your data, and in any case before we first communicate with you or disclose your data to any other party.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement a workflow to identify indirectly-collected data and trigger the Section 8 notice before first use or disclosure.
Notes: Common scenario: lead generation, HR background checks, acquired databases.

Requirement Description: Data Principal has the right to obtain confirmation of processing, summary of personal data, and identification of recipients to whom data has been shared
Plain English Explanation: Users can ask you to confirm whether you process their data, give them a summary of what data you have, and tell them who you've shared it with.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Terms & Conditions, Data Subject Access Request Form
Keywords for Detection: right to access, access request, obtain confirmation, summary of data, personal data summary, confirm processing, what data we hold, data copy, DSAR
Evidence Required: Access request procedure, DSAR response template, Data retrieval mechanism, Response time tracking
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy inform Data Principals of their right to access their personal data and request a summary?
Sample Compliant Language: You have the right to: (a) obtain confirmation from us as to whether we process your personal data; (b) request a summary of the personal data we hold about you; and (c) obtain information about the recipients or categories of recipients to whom your personal data has been shared. To exercise this right, submit a request to our Grievance Officer at [email]. We will respond within [X days] as required under the DPDP Act.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement a DSAR portal or form. Establish internal workflows to retrieve, summarize, and share data within statutory timelines.
Notes: The DPDP Act specifies a response timeline. Track and comply with it.

Requirement Description: Data Principal has the right to request correction of inaccurate or misleading personal data and completion of incomplete personal data
Plain English Explanation: Users can ask you to fix wrong information about them or complete incomplete information.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Terms & Conditions, Correction Request Form, Account Settings
Keywords for Detection: right to correction, correction request, correct inaccurate data, rectify, update data, fix data, amend data, correction of data, misleading data, completion of incomplete data
Evidence Required: Correction request procedure, Data correction workflow, Account edit functionality, Correction audit trail
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy inform Data Principals of their right to request correction of inaccurate or misleading personal data?
Sample Compliant Language: You have the right to request correction of any personal data that is inaccurate, misleading, or incomplete. You may update certain information directly through your [Account Settings] or by submitting a correction request to our Grievance Officer at [email]. Upon receiving your request, we will verify the correction and update our records within [X days].
Remediation Recommendation: Provide self-service data correction in account settings. For non-editable fields, establish a correction request workflow with verification procedures.
Notes: Self-service correction is the best practice. Manual requests should also be accommodated.

DPDP Section Title: Right to Erasure (Right to be Forgotten)
Requirement Description: Data Principal has the right to request erasure of personal data, and the Data Fiduciary must erase data unless retention is necessary for specified purposes or legal obligations
Plain English Explanation: Users can ask you to delete their data. You must delete it unless a law requires you to keep it or it's needed for legal claims.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Terms & Conditions, Erasure Request Form, Account Deletion
Keywords for Detection: right to erasure, right to be forgotten, delete data, erase data, deletion request, data deletion, remove data, erase personal data, account deletion
Evidence Required: Erasure request procedure, Deletion workflow, Deletion confirmation records, Retention exception documentation
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy inform Data Principals of their right to request erasure of personal data?
Sample Compliant Language: You have the right to request erasure of your personal data. You may delete your account through [Account Settings > Delete Account] or submit a deletion request to our Grievance Officer at [email]. Upon receiving your request, we will erase your personal data within [X days], unless retention is necessary for: (a) compliance with a legal obligation; (b) initiation or defence of legal claims; or (c) purposes specified under the DPDP Act.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement a self-service account deletion feature. For manual requests, establish a deletion workflow that handles exceptions for legally-required retention.
Notes: Must be read with Section 12 (erasure). The right to erasure and consent withdrawal both trigger deletion obligations.

Requirement Description: The manner in which Data Principals may exercise their rights must be simple and accessible through designated channels
Plain English Explanation: You must make it easy for users to exercise their rights - not buried in complex procedures or requiring excessive steps.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Help Center, User Dashboard, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: exercise rights, how to exercise, manner of exercising, simple process, accessible, rights request, designated channel, submit request
Evidence Required: Rights request mechanism, User testing of rights exercise flow, Accessibility audit of rights channels
Compliance Test Question: Is the process for exercising rights simple, accessible, and clearly communicated to Data Principals?
Sample Compliant Language: To exercise any of your rights under the DPDP Act, you may: (1) Use our self-service portal at [link]; (2) Email our Grievance Officer at [email]; or (3) Write to us at [postal address]. We have designed these channels to be simple and accessible. We will acknowledge your request within [X days] and respond within [Y days].
Remediation Recommendation: Provide multiple channels for rights requests (online form, email, postal). Test the process for simplicity. Avoid requiring excessive identity verification beyond what is necessary.
Notes: The process must be genuinely simple. Requiring notarized documents or excessive steps may be challenged.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must respond to rights requests within a reasonable timeframe as may be prescribed, or inform the Data Principal of the extension and reasons
Plain English Explanation: You must respond to user requests within the time limit set by rules (or a reasonable time if not yet specified), and tell them if you need more time and why.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Internal SLA Document, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: response time, respond within, timeframe, timeline, reasonable period, prescribed period, extension, delay in response, days to respond
Evidence Required: Internal SLA for rights requests, Response time tracking reports, Extension justification records
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization respond to rights requests within the statutory timeframe and track response times?
Sample Compliant Language: We will respond to your rights request within [30 days] of receipt. If we require additional time due to the complexity or volume of requests, we will inform you of the extension and reasons within [30 days] of your request. We will provide the final response no later than [60 days] from the date of your request.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement a ticketing system to track rights requests with SLA timers. Set up alerts for approaching deadlines.
Notes: The Central Government may prescribe specific timelines through rules. Monitor for notifications.

DPDP Section Title: Nomination of Representative by Data Principal
Requirement Description: Data Principal has the right to nominate any individual who shall exercise rights on their behalf in case of death or incapacity
Plain English Explanation: Users can appoint someone (like a family member) to exercise their data rights if they die or become incapacitated.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Nomination Form, Account Settings
Keywords for Detection: nominate, nomination, representative, exercise rights on behalf, death, incapacity, personal representative, nominee, authorized representative
Evidence Required: Nomination mechanism, Nominee records, Verification procedure for nominees
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy inform Data Principals of their right to nominate a representative to exercise rights on their behalf?
Sample Compliant Language: You have the right to nominate any individual who shall, in the event of your death or incapacity, exercise your rights under this Privacy Policy and the DPDP Act. To nominate a representative, please complete the nomination form available at [link] or contact our Grievance Officer. You may change or revoke your nomination at any time.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement a nomination feature in account settings or provide a downloadable nomination form. Establish verification procedures for nominees.
Notes: Unique to DPDP (not in GDPR). Important for succession planning and elderly users.

DPDP Section Title: Right to Erasure of Personal Data
Requirement Description: Data Principal has the right to request erasure of personal data, and Data Fiduciary must comply unless retention is necessary for specified purposes including legal obligations, legal claims, or as required by law
Plain English Explanation: Users have a standalone right to have their data deleted. You must delete it unless there's a specific legal reason to keep it.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Terms & Conditions, Data Retention Policy
Keywords for Detection: Section 12, right to erasure, erasure of personal data, delete personal data, mandatory erasure, erasure obligation, deletion right
Evidence Required: Erasure request procedure, Deletion workflow logs, Retention schedule for exceptions, Legal basis for retention exceptions
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization have a process to receive, evaluate, and execute erasure requests within statutory timelines?
Sample Compliant Language: Under Section 12 of the DPDP Act, you have the right to request the erasure of your personal data. Upon receiving your request, we will erase your data within [X days], except where retention is necessary for: (i) compliance with any law; (ii) the initiation or defence of legal claims; (iii) performance of any function under any law by the State; or (iv) any other purpose as may be prescribed.
Remediation Recommendation: Build automated erasure workflows. Maintain a retention exception register documenting the legal basis for retaining data despite erasure requests.
Notes: Section 12 creates a statutory right to erasure independent of consent withdrawal. Both paths trigger deletion.

Requirement Description: Every Data Fiduciary must appoint a Grievance Officer and publish their name and contact details on the website or app
Plain English Explanation: You must designate a specific person as Grievance Officer, publish their name, and make their contact details easily available on your platform.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Website, Mobile App, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: grievance officer, appointed, name, contact details, publish, website, app, designated officer, complaint officer, redressal officer
Evidence Required: Grievance Officer appointment letter, Published contact details, Website screenshot showing Grievance Officer information
Compliance Test Question: Is a Grievance Officer appointed and are their name and contact details published on the website/app?
Sample Compliant Language: We have appointed a Grievance Officer to address your concerns regarding personal data processing. Grievance Officer: [Full Name], [Designation]. Address: [Full Postal Address]. Email: [email@company.com]. Phone: [+91-XXXXXXXXXX]. The Grievance Officer's details are published on our website [link] and mobile app [location].
Remediation Recommendation: Appoint a Grievance Officer (can be an existing employee). Publish their name, designation, full address, email, and phone on the website footer, Privacy Policy, and app settings.
Notes: This is a core compliance requirement. Every Data Fiduciary, regardless of size, must appoint a Grievance Officer.

Requirement Description: Every Data Fiduciary must establish an effective grievance redressal mechanism for Data Principals to raise concerns about personal data processing
Plain English Explanation: You must have a proper internal process for handling complaints about how you handle personal data, and tell users how to use it.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Grievance Redressal Policy, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: grievance redressal mechanism, complaint mechanism, raise concerns, dispute resolution, complaint handling, redressal process, concern mechanism, how to raise
Evidence Required: Grievance Redressal Policy, Complaint handling procedure, Complaint register/logs, Resolution tracking records
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization have an established grievance redressal mechanism with documented procedures for handling data protection complaints?
Sample Compliant Language: We have established a grievance redressal mechanism to address your data protection concerns. You may file a complaint by: (1) Emailing our Grievance Officer at [email]; (2) Submitting the complaint form at [link]; or (3) Writing to [postal address]. We will acknowledge your complaint within [2 days] and provide a substantive response within [30 days]. If you are dissatisfied with our response, you may escalate to the Data Protection Board of India.
Remediation Recommendation: Create a Grievance Redressal Policy with defined timelines for acknowledgment, investigation, and resolution. Implement a complaint tracking system.
Notes: The mechanism must be 'effective' - meaning complaints must actually be addressed, not just received.

Requirement Description: If grievance is not resolved satisfactorily, Data Principal has the right to approach the Data Protection Board of India
Plain English Explanation: If a user is not happy with how you handled their complaint, they can escalate to the Data Protection Board (the government body). You must tell them this right.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Grievance Redressal Policy, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: Data Protection Board, escalate, escalation, Board complaint, appeal to Board, regulatory complaint, not satisfied, unresolved grievance
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy clause referencing Board, Escalation procedure, Board contact information maintained current
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy inform Data Principals of their right to approach the Data Protection Board if their grievance is not resolved?
Sample Compliant Language: If you are not satisfied with our response to your grievance, you have the right to file a complaint with the Data Protection Board of India established under Section 19 of the DPDP Act, 2023. The Board is the regulatory authority responsible for enforcing the DPDP Act. [Contact details will be updated once the Board is fully operational.]
Remediation Recommendation: Include a clear escalation path in the grievance redressal section. Monitor the Data Protection Board's operational status and update contact details when available.
Notes: The Data Protection Board is newly established. Keep contact details updated as the Board becomes operational.

Requirement Description: The Grievance Officer must respond to complaints within a reasonable period as may be prescribed by the Central Government
Plain English Explanation: The Grievance Officer must reply to complaints within the timeframe set by government rules (or a reasonable time if not specified).
Applicable Document: Grievance Redressal Policy, Internal SLA
Keywords for Detection: respond within, response timeline, reasonable period, prescribed period, grievance response, acknowledge complaint, resolution time, days to respond
Evidence Required: SLA documentation, Response time metrics, Complaint resolution reports
Compliance Test Question: Does the Grievance Officer respond to complaints within the prescribed or a reasonable timeframe?
Sample Compliant Language: Our Grievance Officer will acknowledge receipt of your complaint within [2 business days] and provide a substantive response within [30 days] of receipt. If the investigation requires additional time, we will inform you of the delay and the expected resolution date.
Remediation Recommendation: Set internal SLAs for complaint acknowledgment (within 48 hours) and resolution (within 30 days). Track and report on response times.
Notes: Pending government notification of specific timelines. Provisional best practice: 30-day resolution.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must implement reasonable technical and organizational measures to protect personal data from unauthorized access, collection, use, disclosure, alteration, or destruction
Plain English Explanation: You must have proper security measures (technical like encryption, and organizational like access controls) to protect user data from hackers, leaks, and unauthorized use.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Information Security Policy, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: security, safeguard, protect, technical measures, organizational measures, unauthorized access, encryption, access control, security policy, data breach prevention, security practices
Evidence Required: Information Security Policy, Encryption implementation records, Access control matrix, Security audit reports, Vulnerability assessment reports
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization implement reasonable technical and organizational security measures to protect personal data?
Sample Compliant Language: We implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures to protect your personal data, including: (a) encryption of data in transit using TLS 1.2+ and at rest using AES-256; (b) role-based access controls limiting data access to authorized personnel; (c) regular security assessments and penetration testing; (d) secure development practices; and (e) employee training on data protection. Despite these measures, no system is completely secure.
Remediation Recommendation: Conduct a security assessment. Implement encryption, access controls, logging, and monitoring. Document all security measures in an Information Security Policy.
Notes: 'Reasonable' is context-dependent. What is reasonable for a bank differs from a small startup. Document your risk assessment.

Requirement Description: In case of a personal data breach, Data Fiduciary must notify the Data Protection Board and affected Data Principals in the prescribed manner and format
Plain English Explanation: If there's a data breach (hack, leak, unauthorized access), you must tell the Data Protection Board and the affected users in the format required by rules.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Breach Response Plan, Incident Response Policy
Keywords for Detection: data breach, personal data breach, breach notification, notify Board, notify affected individuals, security incident, unauthorized access, data leak, breach response, incident notification
Evidence Required: Incident response plan, Breach notification templates, Communication logs, Board notification records
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization have a data breach response plan including procedures to notify the Data Protection Board and affected Data Principals?
Sample Compliant Language: In the event of a personal data breach, we will: (a) assess the nature and scope of the breach; (b) notify the Data Protection Board of India within [the prescribed period - update when notified] in the prescribed format; (c) notify affected Data Principals without undue delay if the breach is likely to result in harm; and (d) take immediate steps to mitigate the breach and prevent recurrence. Breach notifications will include: description of breach, categories of data affected, likely consequences, measures taken, and contact details for further information.
Remediation Recommendation: Create a Data Breach Response Plan with clear escalation paths. Prepare breach notification templates for the Board and affected individuals. Conduct breach response drills.
Notes: Government notification of prescribed format and timeline is pending. Prepare templates in advance.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must register with the Data Protection Board of India and comply with registration requirements as may be prescribed
Plain English Explanation: You may need to register with the Data Protection Board and follow registration requirements that will be specified in rules.
Applicable Document: Registration Certificate, Privacy Policy (if applicable)
Keywords for Detection: registration, register with Board, Data Protection Board registration, enrolled, data fiduciary registration, mandatory registration
Evidence Required: Registration certificate or application, Registration number, Compliance with registration conditions
Compliance Test Question: If registration with the Data Protection Board is prescribed for the entity class, is the registration completed and current?
Sample Compliant Language: [If applicable: We are registered with the Data Protection Board of India under Registration No. [number]. Our registration details can be verified at [link].]
Remediation Recommendation: Monitor government notifications regarding registration requirements. Register promptly when requirements are notified for your entity class.
Notes: Registration requirements are pending government notification. Likely to be phased by entity type and size.

DPDP Section Title: Maintenance of Accurate and Up-to-Date Data
Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must ensure that personal data processed is accurate, complete, and kept up-to-date, especially where decisions affecting the Data Principal are made
Plain English Explanation: You must keep user data accurate and current, especially when using it to make decisions that affect them (like loan approvals or job applications).
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Quality Policy, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: accurate, up-to-date, accurate data, data quality, complete data, correct data, update information, keep current, data accuracy, ensure accuracy
Evidence Required: Data quality procedures, Update mechanisms, Data validation rules, Accuracy audit records
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization take steps to ensure personal data is accurate, complete, and kept up-to-date?
Sample Compliant Language: We take reasonable steps to ensure that the personal data we process is accurate, complete, and current. You are responsible for informing us of any changes to your personal data. We encourage you to review and update your information regularly through your [Account Settings]. Where we use your data for decisions that affect you, we implement additional verification steps to ensure accuracy.
Remediation Recommendation: Provide self-service data update functionality. Implement data validation at collection points. Periodically prompt users to review and update their information.
Notes: Data accuracy obligation is linked to the right to correction. Both support each other.

DPDP Section Title: Deletion of Personal Data When No Longer Necessary
Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must erase personal data as soon as the purpose of collection is fulfilled and retention is no longer necessary for legal or business purposes
Plain English Explanation: You must delete user data when you no longer need it for the original purpose, unless a law requires you to keep it longer.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Retention Policy, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: delete when no longer necessary, purpose fulfilled, erase after purpose, no longer needed, deletion obligation, remove when purpose complete, retention limitation
Evidence Required: Data Retention Policy, Automated deletion schedules, Deletion logs, Purpose fulfillment assessment procedure
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization delete personal data once the purpose of collection is fulfilled, unless retention is legally required?
Sample Compliant Language: We retain your personal data only for as long as necessary to fulfill the purposes for which it was collected. Once the purpose is fulfilled and no legal obligation requires further retention, we will securely delete or anonymize your data. Our retention periods are detailed in the Data Retention section of this Privacy Policy.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement automated data deletion schedules triggered by purpose completion or retention period expiry. Maintain deletion logs as evidence.
Notes: This is a proactive deletion obligation, distinct from the reactive erasure right under Section 12.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must publish the business contact information of a person who can answer questions about data processing practices
Plain English Explanation: You must provide contact details of someone who can answer questions about how you handle data - this could be the Grievance Officer or another designated person.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Website Contact Page, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: business contact, contact information, contact person, data protection contact, questions about data, query contact, data practices contact, who to contact
Evidence Required: Published contact information, Website/app screenshot showing contact details
Compliance Test Question: Is business contact information published for Data Principals to ask questions about data processing practices?
Sample Compliant Language: For questions about our data processing practices, you may contact: [Name/Department], [Email], [Phone], [Address]. We aim to respond to all inquiries within [X business days].
Remediation Recommendation: Designate a contact person or team for data protection queries. Publish their contact details prominently on the website and in the Privacy Policy.
Notes: This can be the Grievance Officer or a separate contact. The key is accessibility and responsiveness.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment for processing that involves high risk to the rights and freedoms of Data Principals
Plain English Explanation: Before doing any processing that could seriously affect people's rights (like large-scale profiling, sensitive data processing), you must assess the privacy risks and how to mitigate them.
Applicable Document: DPIA Report, Privacy Policy (reference)
Keywords for Detection: DPIA, impact assessment, data protection impact, risk assessment, high risk processing, privacy impact, prior consultation, privacy risk
Evidence Required: DPIA report, Risk register, Mitigation measures documentation, Board consultation records (if required)
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments for high-risk processing activities?
Sample Compliant Language: We conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for processing activities that may pose a high risk to your rights and freedoms, including [specify: e.g., systematic profiling, large-scale processing, use of new technologies]. Our DPIAs identify risks and document mitigation measures. Where required, we consult the Data Protection Board prior to such processing.
Remediation Recommendation: Identify high-risk processing activities. Conduct DPIAs before initiating such processing. Maintain DPIA records. Consult the Board where required.
Notes: High-risk criteria will be specified in rules. As a precaution, conduct DPIAs for sensitive processing, profiling, and large-scale operations.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must maintain accurate and up-to-date records of processing activities including purpose, categories of data, recipients, and cross-border transfers
Plain English Explanation: You must keep an internal record (like a register) of all your data processing activities - what data you process, why, who you share it with, and whether it goes outside India.
Applicable Document: Records of Processing Activities (ROPA), Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: records of processing, ROPA, processing records, register of processing, processing activities, maintain records, data processing inventory, data map
Evidence Required: ROPA document, Data processing inventory, Data flow maps, Update logs
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization maintain accurate records of processing activities as required under Section 11(6)?
Sample Compliant Language: [Internal document - not required in Privacy Policy] We maintain comprehensive records of our processing activities (ROPA) documenting: processing purposes, categories of personal data, categories of recipients, cross-border transfers, retention periods, and security measures. These records are made available to the Data Protection Board upon request.
Remediation Recommendation: Create and maintain a ROPA document. Update it whenever processing activities change. Make it available for regulatory inspection.
Notes: ROPA is an internal compliance document. Not required to be published, but must be available to the Board.

Requirement Description: Significant Data Fiduciaries and other notified categories must appoint a Data Protection Officer resident in India
Plain English Explanation: If you're a Significant Data Fiduciary (or another category notified by the government), you must appoint a Data Protection Officer who lives in India.
Mandatory or Optional: Conditional (Significant Data Fiduciaries and notified categories)
Applicable Document: DPO Appointment Letter, Privacy Policy, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: data protection officer, DPO, appointed DPO, resident in India, India-based DPO, significant data fiduciary DPO, DPO requirement
Evidence Required: DPO appointment letter, DPO residency proof, DPO qualifications, Published DPO contact details
Compliance Test Question: If the organization is a Significant Data Fiduciary, has a Data Protection Officer been appointed who is resident in India?
Sample Compliant Language: [If applicable to SDFs] We have appointed a Data Protection Officer (DPO) resident in India as required under the DPDP Act. Data Protection Officer: [Name], [Qualifications]. Contact: [Email], [Phone], [Address]. The DPO oversees our data protection strategy and ensures compliance with the DPDP Act.
Remediation Recommendation: Assess whether you qualify as a Significant Data Fiduciary. If yes, appoint a qualified DPO resident in India and publish their contact details.
Notes: DPO requirement applies to Significant Data Fiduciaries. The Board may extend this to other categories.

Requirement Description: Significant Data Fiduciaries must appoint an independent data auditor to evaluate compliance with the Act
Plain English Explanation: If you're a Significant Data Fiduciary, you must hire an independent auditor to check if you're complying with the DPDP Act.
Applicable Document: Data Auditor Appointment, Audit Report, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: data auditor, independent auditor, compliance auditor, audit compliance, significant data fiduciary audit, data protection audit, auditor appointment
Evidence Required: Auditor appointment agreement, Auditor independence declaration, Audit reports, Board submission of audit reports
Compliance Test Question: If the organization is a Significant Data Fiduciary, has an independent data auditor been appointed and are audits conducted periodically?
Sample Compliant Language: [If applicable to SDFs] As a Significant Data Fiduciary, we have appointed [Auditor Name], an independent data auditor, to evaluate our compliance with the DPDP Act. Audit reports are submitted to the Data Protection Board as required. Our most recent audit was conducted on [date].
Remediation Recommendation: If you're a Significant Data Fiduciary, identify and appoint an independent auditor with data protection expertise. Conduct annual compliance audits.
Notes: Auditor must be independent. Cannot be an employee or have conflicts of interest.

Requirement Description: Personal data must not be retained beyond the period necessary to satisfy the purpose for which it was collected and processed
Plain English Explanation: You can only keep data for as long as you need it for the original purpose you collected it. Once that purpose is done, you must delete it.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Retention Policy, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: retain only as long as necessary, purpose limitation retention, not retain beyond purpose, retention limitation, data minimization retention, retention period tied to purpose
Evidence Required: Data Retention Policy, Retention schedule, Automated deletion configuration, Retention period audit
Compliance Test Question: Is personal data retained only for the period necessary to fulfill the purpose of collection?
Sample Compliant Language: We retain your personal data only for as long as necessary to fulfill the purposes for which it was collected, including for the purposes of satisfying any legal, accounting, or reporting requirements. Once the purpose is fulfilled and no legal obligation requires retention, your data is securely deleted or anonymized.
Remediation Recommendation: Create a retention schedule mapping each data category to its purpose and retention period. Implement technical controls to enforce automatic deletion at retention expiry.
Notes: Purpose limitation and data minimization are core DPDP principles. Retention must be tied to purpose, not arbitrary time periods.

Requirement Description: Personal data may be retained beyond purpose fulfillment if such retention is necessary for compliance with any law or court order
Plain English Explanation: If a law requires you to keep data for a specific period (like tax records for 7 years), you can retain it even after the original purpose is fulfilled.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Retention Policy, Legal Hold Policy
Keywords for Detection: legal obligation retention, required by law, court order retention, statutory retention, mandatory retention, legal hold, regulatory requirement, law requires retention
Evidence Required: Retention schedule citing legal basis, Legal requirement register, Legal hold records
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization document the legal basis for retaining data beyond purpose fulfillment and maintain a legal retention schedule?
Sample Compliant Language: In certain circumstances, we are legally required to retain personal data beyond the fulfillment of the original purpose. This includes: (a) Financial records retained for 7 years under the Income Tax Act; (b) Employment records retained as required by labour laws; (c) Data subject to litigation hold; and (d) Any other retention required by applicable law or court order. We maintain a legal retention schedule documenting the specific law and retention period applicable to each data category.
Remediation Recommendation: Create a legal retention schedule that identifies the specific law requiring retention for each data category. Implement legal hold procedures for litigation-related retention.
Notes: Must identify the specific law. Vague references to 'legal requirements' are insufficient.

Requirement Description: Upon erasure request or consent withdrawal, data must be erased unless retention is necessary for legal claims, legal obligations, or State functions
Plain English Explanation: Even when someone asks you to delete their data or withdraws consent, you can keep it if needed for legal disputes, legal compliance, or government functions.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Retention Policy, Erasure Request Procedure
Keywords for Detection: exception to erasure, legal claims retention, legal obligation exception, state function retention, retain despite erasure, erasure exception, deletion exception
Evidence Required: Erasure exception register, Legal basis documentation for each exception, Retention schedule for erasure-exception data
Compliance Test Question: When honoring erasure requests, does the organization properly evaluate and document exceptions for legally-required retention?
Sample Compliant Language: While you have the right to request erasure of your personal data, we may need to retain certain data despite your request where necessary for: (a) compliance with a legal obligation; (b) the initiation or defence of legal claims; (c) performance of any State function; or (d) any other purpose as may be prescribed. Where we retain data under an exception, we will inform you of the specific exception and the retention period.
Remediation Recommendation: Create an erasure exception assessment procedure. For each erasure request, document the specific exception claimed and the legal basis. Restrict further processing of retained data to the exception purpose only.
Notes: This is a heavily-litigated area. Document each exception decision carefully.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must disclose categories of recipients to whom personal data may be transferred or shared
Plain English Explanation: You must tell users what types of organizations you share their data with - like cloud providers, analytics companies, payment processors, etc.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Sharing Agreement
Keywords for Detection: recipients, data sharing, shared with, third parties, disclose to, transferred to, categories of recipients, who we share, data disclosure
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy with recipient categories, Data sharing inventory, Processing agreements with recipients
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy disclose the categories of recipients to whom personal data is shared?
Sample Compliant Language: We may share your personal data with the following categories of recipients: (a) Cloud Infrastructure Providers [e.g., AWS, Azure]; (b) Payment Gateway Providers; (c) Customer Communication Platforms; (d) Analytics and Improvement Partners; (e) Group Companies and Affiliates; (f) Legal and Regulatory Authorities; and (g) Professional Advisors. We have data processing agreements in place with all service providers who process personal data on our behalf.
Remediation Recommendation: Inventory all data sharing relationships. Categorize recipients by type. Disclose categories in the Privacy Policy. Maintain data processing agreements with all processors.
Notes: Categories of recipients are sufficient. Individual company names are recommended but not mandatory.

Requirement Description: When engaging Data Processors, Data Fiduciary must ensure processors implement appropriate security safeguards and process data only as instructed
Plain English Explanation: If you use third-party service providers (like cloud hosting, email services), you must make sure they also protect the data adequately and only use it as you direct them.
Applicable Document: Data Processing Agreement, Privacy Policy, Vendor Security Assessment
Keywords for Detection: data processor, third party security, processor obligations, vendor security, service provider data protection, sub-processor, data processing agreement, DPA
Evidence Required: Data Processing Agreements with all processors, Vendor security assessment reports, Processor audit rights exercised
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization have data processing agreements with all Data Processors requiring appropriate security measures?
Sample Compliant Language: We engage trusted third-party service providers (Data Processors) to perform functions on our behalf.
