We require all Data Processors to: (a) process personal data only for the purposes we specify; (b) implement security measures at least as protective as those we implement; (c) notify us of any data breaches; (d) assist us in responding to rights requests; and (e) return or delete personal data upon termination of services. We have Data Processing Agreements in place with all our processors.
Remediation Recommendation: Inventory all Data Processors. Execute Data Processing Agreements with each. Conduct periodic vendor security assessments.
Notes: You remain liable for processor actions. Due diligence in processor selection is essential.

Requirement Description: If using a Consent Manager, Data Fiduciary must ensure the Consent Manager is registered, acts as Data Principal's agent, and does not process data for own purposes
Plain English Explanation: If you use a third-party tool to manage user consent, that tool must be registered, act on behalf of users, and not use their data for its own purposes.
Applicable Document: Consent Manager Agreement, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: consent manager, registered consent manager, acts as agent, does not process own purpose, CMP agreement, consent manager registration
Evidence Required: Consent Manager registration certificate, Consent Manager agreement, Audit of Consent Manager practices
Compliance Test Question: If using a Consent Manager, is there a valid agreement ensuring the Consent Manager acts as the Data Principal's agent?
Sample Compliant Language: We use [Consent Manager Name], a Consent Manager registered under the DPDP Act. The Consent Manager acts as your agent to manage your consent preferences and does not process your personal data for its own purposes. Our agreement with [Consent Manager Name] ensures that your consent choices are honored across all participating Data Fiduciaries.
Remediation Recommendation: Verify Consent Manager registration status. Ensure the agreement specifies the agent relationship and prohibits independent data processing. Audit periodically.
Notes: Consent Managers occupy a unique position. They must be independent and act as user agents, not as service providers.

Requirement Description: Personal data may be transferred to countries or territories outside India that the Central Government has notified as providing adequate protection
Plain English Explanation: You can transfer data to countries that the Indian government has approved as having good enough data protection laws.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Cross-Border Transfer Policy, Data Transfer Agreement
Keywords for Detection: cross-border transfer, transfer outside India, international transfer, notified country, adequate protection, approved jurisdiction, data export, overseas transfer
Evidence Required: Government notification of approved countries, Transfer impact assessment, Transfer mechanism documentation
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization transfer personal data only to countries notified by the Central Government as providing adequate protection, or through approved transfer mechanisms?
Sample Compliant Language: Your personal data may be transferred to and stored in countries outside India. We ensure such transfers comply with Section 16 of the DPDP Act by: (a) transferring only to countries notified by the Government of India as providing adequate data protection; or (b) implementing appropriate transfer safeguards as approved under the DPDP Act. A list of countries to which we transfer data is available upon request.
Remediation Recommendation: Monitor government notifications of approved countries. Map all cross-border data flows. Implement approved transfer mechanisms for non-approved countries.
Notes: The government has not yet notified the list of approved countries. Prepare for compliance when the list is published.

Requirement Description: Cross-border transfers may be made through such mechanisms as may be prescribed by the Central Government, including standard contractual clauses or intra-group schemes
Plain English Explanation: If transferring to a country not on the approved list, you must use a mechanism approved by the government, like standard contractual clauses (SCCs) or binding corporate rules.
Applicable Document: Standard Contractual Clauses, Intra-Group Transfer Agreement, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: standard contractual clauses, SCC, intra-group scheme, binding corporate rules, BCR, transfer mechanism, transfer safeguard, approved mechanism, contractual safeguard
Evidence Required: Executed SCCs or intra-group agreements, Transfer impact assessment, Government notification of approved mechanisms
Compliance Test Question: For transfers to countries not on the approved list, does the organization implement prescribed transfer mechanisms such as standard contractual clauses?
Sample Compliant Language: For transfers to countries not notified as having adequate protection, we implement appropriate safeguards including: (a) Standard Contractual Clause (SCC) agreements approved under the DPDP Act; (b) Intra-group data transfer agreements with adequate protection clauses; and (c) any other mechanism as may be prescribed by the Central Government. These safeguards ensure your data receives protection comparable to that under Indian law.
Remediation Recommendation: Identify cross-border transfers to non-approved countries. Execute SCCs or other approved mechanisms. Conduct transfer impact assessments.
Notes: SCC templates and approved mechanisms are pending government notification. Prepare draft agreements in advance.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must provide notice to Data Principals regarding cross-border transfers including destination countries and safeguards implemented
Plain English Explanation: You must tell users when their data is sent outside India, which countries it goes to, and what protections are in place.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Cross-Border Transfer Notice, Consent Notice
Keywords for Detection: transfer notice, cross-border notice, destination country, countries where data is transferred, transfer disclosure, international disclosure, where data is stored
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy with cross-border disclosure, Transfer register, Destination country list
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy disclose cross-border transfers including destination countries and implemented safeguards?
Sample Compliant Language: We may transfer your personal data to the following countries: [list or categories]. Transfers are made [to notified countries / using Standard Contractual Clauses / under intra-group agreements]. If you have questions about cross-border transfers, please contact our Grievance Officer.
Remediation Recommendation: Create a cross-border transfer register. Disclose transfers in the Privacy Policy. Ensure consent notices mention cross-border transfers at the time of collection.
Notes: Cross-border transfer disclosure must appear both in the Privacy Policy and in the notice at the time of collection (Section 8).

Requirement Description: Central Government may notify any Data Fiduciary as a Significant Data Fiduciary based on volume and sensitivity of data, risk to Data Principals, potential impact on sovereignty and security, and use of new technology
Plain English Explanation: The government can designate certain organizations as 'Significant Data Fiduciaries' based on how much sensitive data they handle, the risk they pose, and whether they use new technologies. These entities face additional compliance obligations.
Applicable Document: Government Notification, Self-Assessment, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: significant data fiduciary, SDF, notified entity, government notification, classification criteria, volume of data, sensitive data, new technology
Evidence Required: Self-assessment against SDF criteria, Government notification response, Board direction records
Compliance Test Question: Has the organization assessed whether it qualifies as a Significant Data Fiduciary and responded to any government notification?
Sample Compliant Language: [Internal assessment document only - not required in Privacy Policy] We have assessed our operations against the Significant Data Fiduciary criteria under Section 12(1) of the DPDP Act. Our assessment considers: (a) volume and sensitivity of personal data processed; (b) risk of harm to Data Principals; (c) potential impact on electoral democracy, State security, and public order; and (d) use of new technologies. We will [comply with SDF obligations / are not currently classified as an SDF].
Remediation Recommendation: Conduct a self-assessment against SDF criteria. Monitor government notifications. If designated as SDF, immediately implement all SDF-specific obligations.
Notes: SDF criteria are broad and discretionary. The government can notify any entity it deems significant.

Requirement Description: Significant Data Fiduciaries must conduct periodic independent audits of their data processing practices and submit audit reports to the Data Protection Board
Plain English Explanation: If you're a Significant Data Fiduciary, you must regularly get an independent auditor to check your data handling and report to the government.
Applicable Document: Audit Report, Audit Plan, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: periodic audit, independent audit, data audit, audit report, submit to Board, SDF audit, significant data fiduciary audit, compliance audit
Evidence Required: Audit reports, Auditor independence declarations, Board submission confirmations, Audit plan and schedule
Compliance Test Question: If an SDF, does the organization conduct periodic independent audits and submit reports to the Data Protection Board?
Sample Compliant Language: [If applicable: As a Significant Data Fiduciary, we conduct independent data protection audits [annually/periodically]. Our most recent audit was completed on [date] by [Auditor Name]. Audit reports are submitted to the Data Protection Board of India as required under Section 12(2) of the DPDP Act.]
Remediation Recommendation: If SDF, establish an audit schedule (recommended: annual). Appoint an independent auditor. Submit reports to the Board within prescribed timelines.
Notes: Audits must be genuinely independent. Board may specify audit scope, frequency, and reporting format through rules.

DPDP Section Title: SDF Additional Obligations - Data Protection Impact Assessment
Requirement Description: Significant Data Fiduciaries must conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments for prescribed processing activities and submit assessments to the Data Protection Board
Plain English Explanation: If you're a Significant Data Fiduciary, you must do privacy impact assessments for high-risk processing and share them with the government.
Applicable Document: DPIA Report, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: DPIA, impact assessment, high risk processing, SDF DPIA, submit to Board, prior consultation, privacy impact assessment, risk assessment
Evidence Required: DPIA reports, Board submission records, Risk mitigation documentation, Prior consultation records
Compliance Test Question: If an SDF, does the organization conduct DPIAs for prescribed processing activities and submit them to the Data Protection Board?
Sample Compliant Language: [If applicable: As a Significant Data Fiduciary, we conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for [list: e.g., large-scale profiling, use of automated decision-making, processing of sensitive personal data]. Our DPIAs identify risks to Data Principals and document mitigation measures. DPIAs are submitted to the Data Protection Board [before processing begins / as required].]
Remediation Recommendation: If SDF, establish a DPIA protocol. Identify processing activities requiring DPIA. Conduct assessments before initiating processing. Submit to Board as prescribed.
Notes: SDF DPIAs may require prior Board consultation, not just submission. Monitor rules for specific requirements.

DPDP Section Title: SDF Additional Obligations - Periodic Data Protection Compliance Report
Requirement Description: Significant Data Fiduciaries must prepare and submit periodic reports on data protection compliance to the Data Protection Board
Plain English Explanation: If you're a Significant Data Fiduciary, you must regularly report to the government about your data protection compliance status.
Applicable Document: Compliance Report, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: compliance report, periodic report, submit to Board, SDF reporting, data protection report, compliance status, regular reporting
Evidence Required: Compliance reports, Board submission confirmations, Reporting schedule, Compliance metrics
Compliance Test Question: If an SDF, does the organization prepare and submit periodic data protection compliance reports to the Data Protection Board?
Sample Compliant Language: [If applicable: As a Significant Data Fiduciary, we prepare and submit periodic data protection compliance reports to the Data Protection Board of India as required under Section 12(4) of the DPDP Act. Our reports cover: data processing activities, security incidents, rights requests handled, and compliance status.]
Remediation Recommendation: If SDF, establish a compliance reporting calendar. Prepare templates covering required metrics. Submit reports within prescribed timelines.
Notes: Reporting format and frequency will be specified in rules. Prepare systems to capture required metrics.

Requirement Description: Personal data must be processed only for the purpose specified at the time of collection and for which consent was obtained, or for a purpose compatible with the original purpose
Plain English Explanation: You can only use data for the reason you told the user about when you collected it. If you want to use it for something else, you need fresh consent.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Terms & Conditions, Consent Form
Keywords for Detection: purpose, purpose of processing, specified purpose, compatible purpose, further processing, new purpose, secondary use, purpose limitation, not use for other purposes
Evidence Required: Purpose specification in Privacy Policy, Consent records showing purpose-specific consent, Purpose change procedure, Fresh consent workflow for new purposes
Compliance Test Question: Is personal data processed only for the purposes specified in the Privacy Policy and for which consent was obtained?
Sample Compliant Language: We process your personal data only for the specific purposes disclosed to you at the time of collection and for which you have provided consent. If we need to process your data for a new purpose not compatible with the original purpose, we will notify you and seek your fresh consent before such processing begins. Our current processing purposes are: [list purposes with specific descriptions].
Remediation Recommendation: Create a purpose inventory mapping each data processing activity to its stated purpose. Implement technical controls preventing use for unstated purposes. Establish a 'new purpose' workflow requiring fresh consent.
Notes: Purpose limitation is a core data protection principle. Secondary processing for compatible purposes is permitted but 'compatible' is narrowly interpreted.

DPDP Section Title: Fresh Consent for New or Incompatible Purposes
Requirement Description: Where processing for a new purpose is not compatible with the original purpose for which consent was obtained, fresh consent must be obtained
Plain English Explanation: If you want to use data for something completely different from what you originally said, you must ask the user for permission again.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Fresh Consent Notice, Consent Form
Keywords for Detection: fresh consent, new purpose, incompatible purpose, secondary processing, further processing, additional consent, re-consent, purpose change, updated consent
Evidence Required: Fresh consent workflow, Consent records for new purposes, Purpose compatibility assessment documentation
Compliance Test Question: When processing personal data for a new or incompatible purpose, is fresh consent obtained from the Data Principal?
Sample Compliant Language: If we wish to process your personal data for a new purpose that is not compatible with the original purpose for which you provided consent, we will notify you via [email/app notification] and request your fresh consent. You are not obligated to provide consent for the new purpose, and your existing services will not be affected by your decision.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement a purpose compatibility assessment procedure. For incompatible new purposes, trigger a fresh consent workflow before any processing begins.
Notes: Compatible purposes (e.g., analytics for service improvement related to original service delivery) may not require fresh consent. Document your compatibility analysis.

Requirement Description: Personal data of children must be processed only for purposes for which verifiable parental consent was obtained, and not for tracking or targeted advertising
Plain English Explanation: For children, you can only use data for what the parent agreed to. You cannot use it for tracking or advertising even if the parent somehow agreed.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Children's Privacy Notice, Parental Consent Form
Keywords for Detection: children purpose limitation, child data purpose, parental consent purpose, not for tracking children, not for advertising children, limited purpose children
Evidence Required: Children's data purpose inventory, Parental consent records showing purpose-specific consent, Technical controls blocking unauthorized purposes
Compliance Test Question: Is children's personal data processed only for the specific purposes covered by verifiable parental consent, with tracking and advertising prohibited?
Sample Compliant Language: Children's personal data is processed solely for the specific purposes for which verifiable parental consent was obtained. We do not process children's data for tracking, behavioral monitoring, or targeted advertising purposes, even if included in broader consent. Processing purposes for children's data are strictly limited to: [list permitted purposes].
Remediation Recommendation: Flag child accounts in your system. Implement technical restrictions preventing processing for prohibited purposes (tracking, advertising) regardless of consent scope.
Notes: The prohibition on tracking and targeted advertising for children is absolute under Section 6(2).

Requirement Description: Only personal data necessary for the specified purpose may be collected. Collection must be limited to what is adequate, relevant, and necessary.
Plain English Explanation: You can only collect data you actually need for the specific purpose. Don't collect extra 'just in case' data.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Data Collection Form, Terms & Conditions
Suggested Clause Name: What Data We Collect and Why
Keywords for Detection: necessary, adequate, relevant, data minimization, minimum necessary, only collect what is needed, limited collection, collection limitation, proportionate
Evidence Required: Data inventory showing purpose-necessity linkage, Field-level justification for collection forms, Data minimization audit
Compliance Test Question: Is personal data collection limited to what is adequate, relevant, and necessary for the specified purpose?
Sample Compliant Language: We collect only the personal data that is adequate, relevant, and necessary for the purposes specified in this Privacy Policy. We do not collect personal data that is not required for our services. For example, we collect [name and email] to create your account, but we do not collect [government ID] unless legally required for [specific purpose].
Remediation Recommendation: Audit all data collection points. Remove non-essential fields. Document the justification for each data element collected. Implement 'privacy by default' in form design.
Notes: Data minimization reduces breach impact and compliance burden. Collecting unnecessary data is a common violation.

DPDP Section Title: Notice for Data Collected from Third Parties
Requirement Description: When personal data is not collected directly from the Data Principal, notice must be provided within a reasonable period and before first disclosure to another recipient
Plain English Explanation: If you get data about someone from another source, you must tell them quickly and before you share it with anyone else.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Indirect Collection Notice, Third-Party Data Receipt Procedure
Keywords for Detection: indirect collection, from other sources, third party collection, not collected directly, received from another, sourced externally, acquired data
Evidence Required: Indirect collection notice template, Third-party data intake log, Notice delivery tracking
Compliance Test Question: When personal data is received from third parties, is notice provided to the Data Principal before first use or disclosure?
Sample Compliant Language: We may receive personal data about you from third parties such as [business partners, referral sources, public databases]. When we receive your data from such sources, we will provide you with the notice required under Section 8 of the DPDP Act within [5 business days] and in any case before we first use your data or disclose it to any other party.
Remediation Recommendation: Tag indirectly-collected data in your systems. Trigger an automated notice workflow upon data import. Block disclosure until notice is delivered.
Notes: Common scenarios: lead generation, recruitment agencies, business acquisitions, referral programs.

DPDP Section Title: Consent Must Be Obtained Before Collection
Requirement Description: Consent must be obtained before or at the time of collection of personal data. No processing may occur before consent is obtained.
Plain English Explanation: You must get permission before you collect any data. You cannot collect data first and get consent later.
Applicable Document: Consent Form, Registration Flow, Privacy Policy, Cookie Banner
Keywords for Detection: before collection, prior to collection, consent before, obtain consent first, no collection without consent, pre-collection consent, consent prerequisite
Evidence Required: User flow showing consent before data entry, Technical controls blocking collection without consent, Timestamp records showing consent precedes collection
Compliance Test Question: Is consent technically enforced before personal data collection occurs, with no data collected prior to consent?
Sample Compliant Language: We obtain your consent before collecting any personal data. Our [registration forms/data collection interfaces] are designed to ensure that you provide consent before entering any personal information. Technical controls prevent data submission until consent is confirmed.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement technical controls that block data collection until consent is obtained. Audit user flows to ensure no pre-consent data collection occurs.
Notes: Cookie banners that load tracking cookies before consent are common violators. Implement 'consent-first' architecture.

DPDP Section Title: Privacy Policy Must Be Clear, Concise, and Accessible
Requirement Description: The notice (Privacy Policy) must be clear, concise, easily accessible, and available in English or a Schedule VIII language
Plain English Explanation: Your Privacy Policy must be easy to understand, not full of legal jargon, easy to find on your website/app, and available in English or Indian languages.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Website Design, Mobile App Design
Keywords for Detection: clear, concise, accessible, plain language, easy to understand, simple language, readable, transparent, available in language, schedule VIII
Evidence Required: Readability assessment (Flesch score), Website accessibility audit, Language availability verification, User testing results
Compliance Test Question: Is the Privacy Policy written in clear, concise language, easily accessible from all pages, and available in the required languages?
Sample Compliant Language: This Privacy Policy is written in plain language to ensure that you can understand how we handle your personal data. It is accessible from every page of our website and app. If you need this policy in any other language or format, please contact our Grievance Officer.
Remediation Recommendation: Simplify Privacy Policy to Flesch Reading Ease 50+. Add prominent link in website footer and app settings. Consider translations in major Indian languages.
Notes: Readability tools: Hemingway Editor, Grammarly, Microsoft Word Flesch score. Target: average Indian internet user.

DPDP Section Title: Privacy Policy Must Be Complete and Comprehensive
Requirement Description: The Privacy Policy must cover all items specified in Section 8: data collected, purpose, recipient categories, retention, rights, grievance mechanism, and cross-border transfers
Plain English Explanation: Your Privacy Policy must include all the specific items required by law - not just some of them.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: comprehensive, complete privacy policy, all required elements, section 8 compliance, full disclosure, complete notice, all items
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy checklist against Section 8 requirements, Gap analysis report
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy contain all elements required under Section 8 of the DPDP Act?
Sample Compliant Language: N/A - This is a document-level assessment, not a specific clause.
Remediation Recommendation: Conduct a gap analysis of the Privacy Policy against all Section 8(1)-8(7) requirements. Fill all gaps. Use this compliance matrix as a checklist.
Related Controls: DPDP-PN-001, DPDP-PN-002, DPDP-PN-003, DPDP-PN-004, DPDP-PN-005, DPDP-PN-006
Notes: This control serves as the umbrella completeness check. Each sub-requirement has its own dedicated control.

DPDP Section Title: Privacy Policy Must Be Kept Up to Date
Requirement Description: The notice (Privacy Policy) must be kept up to date with any material changes to processing practices, data recipients, or rights
Plain English Explanation: You must keep your Privacy Policy current. When something important changes (like starting to share data with a new partner), you must update the policy.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Change Management Process
Keywords for Detection: changes to policy, update privacy policy, revised, modified, last updated, effective date, material changes, policy update, we may update, we may change
Evidence Required: Privacy Policy version history, Change log, Update notification records, Effective date tracking
Compliance Test Question: Is the Privacy Policy kept up to date, with material changes communicated to Data Principals?
Sample Compliant Language: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time to reflect changes in our practices, legal requirements, or operational needs. We will notify you of material changes by [email/app notification/website banner] at least [15 days] before the changes take effect. The 'Last Updated' date at the top of this policy indicates when it was last revised. We encourage you to review this policy periodically.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement a Privacy Policy change management process. For material changes, notify users in advance and obtain fresh consent where required. Track all versions.
Notes: Material changes to processing purpose require fresh consent, not just notice. Distinguish between updates and material changes.

DPDP Section Title: Consent Notice Must Be Distinct from General Privacy Policy
Requirement Description: The notice provided at the time of consent must be specific and distinct, not merely a general reference to the Privacy Policy
Plain English Explanation: When asking for consent, you must provide a focused notice with key information, not just say 'see our Privacy Policy'.
Applicable Document: Consent Notice, Consent Form, Registration Page
Keywords for Detection: at the time of consent, notice with consent, specific notice, consent notice, not merely reference, alongside consent, when seeking consent, consent-time notice
Evidence Required: Consent notice showing all Section 5(6) elements, Consent flow UI, Notice delivery mechanism
Compliance Test Question: Is a specific notice with all required elements provided at the time of seeking consent, rather than just a link to the Privacy Policy?
Sample Compliant Language: Before you provide consent, please review this important notice: We are [Company Name]. We will use your data for [specific purposes]. We may share it with [recipient categories]. We will keep it for [retention period]. You have rights including access, correction, and erasure. For concerns, contact [Grievance Officer details]. See our full Privacy Policy for complete details [link].
Remediation Recommendation: Design a concise consent notice that includes all Section 5(6) requirements. Display it prominently alongside the consent mechanism, not buried in a link.
Notes: A generic 'I have read the Privacy Policy' checkbox does NOT satisfy the Section 5(6) notice requirement. A specific notice is mandatory.

DPDP Section Title: Terms & Conditions Must Reference Privacy Policy and DPDP Obligations
Requirement Description: Terms & Conditions must include references to the Privacy Policy, data protection obligations, and user rights under the DPDP Act
Plain English Explanation: Your Terms of Service should mention your Privacy Policy, acknowledge data protection obligations, and confirm that users have rights under Indian data protection law.
Applicable Document: Terms & Conditions, User Agreement
Suggested Clause Name: Data Protection and Privacy Policy Reference
Keywords for Detection: privacy policy, data protection, DPDP Act, Digital Personal Data Protection, personal data, user rights, data rights, privacy obligations
Evidence Required: Terms & Conditions with privacy references, Cross-reference verification between T&C and Privacy Policy
Compliance Test Question: Do the Terms & Conditions contain a clear reference to the Privacy Policy and acknowledge data protection obligations under the DPDP Act?
Sample Compliant Language: Your use of our services is governed by these Terms & Conditions and our Privacy Policy, which is incorporated by reference. Our Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, and protect your personal data in compliance with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act). We encourage you to read the Privacy Policy carefully. By agreeing to these Terms, you acknowledge that you have read and understood our Privacy Policy.
Remediation Recommendation: Add a dedicated data protection clause in the T&C. Ensure the Privacy Policy is incorporated by reference. Cross-reference DPDP Act compliance.
Notes: Courts view T&C and Privacy Policy as a single contractual framework. Inconsistencies between the two create legal risk.

DPDP Section Title: Terms & Conditions Must Not Override Consent Requirements
Requirement Description: Terms & Conditions must not contain clauses that purport to obtain blanket consent, waive consent requirements, or override DPDP consent standards
Plain English Explanation: You cannot use your Terms of Service to get automatic permission for all data uses. Each specific purpose must have its own proper consent.
Applicable Document: Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: you consent to, you agree that we may use, blanket consent, by using you consent, deemed consent, implied consent, you authorize us to, automatic consent, waive rights
Evidence Required: T&C review report, Redline of prohibited clauses, Clean version with compliant language
Compliance Test Question: Do the Terms & Conditions contain any blanket consent clauses, implied consent provisions, or clauses purporting to waive DPDP rights?
Sample Compliant Language: [Clause to REMOVE]: 'By using our services, you consent to our collection and use of your data as described herein.' [Replace with]: 'We process your personal data based on your explicit, specific, and informed consent as required by the DPDP Act, 2023. Your consent is obtained separately from these Terms through our designated consent mechanism. Nothing in these Terms overrides your rights under the DPDP Act.'
Remediation Recommendation: Audit T&C for blanket/implied consent language. Replace with explicit consent references. Add a clause preserving DPDP rights.
Notes: Many existing T&C contain non-compliant blanket consent language. This is a common remediation target.

DPDP Section Title: Terms & Conditions Must Reference User Rights and Grievance Mechanism
Requirement Description: Terms & Conditions must inform users of their rights under the DPDP Act and provide information about the grievance redressal mechanism
Plain English Explanation: Your Terms should tell users about their rights (access, correction, deletion) and how to file complaints.
Applicable Document: Terms & Conditions, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: your rights, data principal rights, right to access, right to correction, right to erasure, grievance officer, complain, complaint mechanism, DPDP rights
Evidence Required: T&C with rights clause, Cross-reference to Privacy Policy rights section, Grievance mechanism reference
Compliance Test Question: Do the Terms & Conditions reference Data Principal rights and the grievance redressal mechanism?
Sample Compliant Language: Under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, you have the following rights regarding your personal data: (a) Right to access; (b) Right to correction and completion; (c) Right to erasure; (d) Right to grievance redressal; and (e) Right to nominate a representative. For details on exercising these rights, please refer to our Privacy Policy [link] or contact our Grievance Officer at [contact details].
Remediation Recommendation: Add a user rights summary clause in the T&C. Cross-reference the detailed rights description in the Privacy Policy. Include Grievance Officer contact details.
Notes: While T&C should reference rights, the detailed description belongs in the Privacy Policy. Avoid duplicating - cross-reference instead.

DPDP Section Title: Terms & Conditions Must Include Age Declaration for Children
Requirement Description: Terms & Conditions must require users to declare they are 18+ or have parental consent, and include provisions restricting children under 18 from using services without supervision
Plain English Explanation: Your Terms should require users to confirm they're 18+ or have parent permission, and restrict children from using services unsupervised if appropriate.
Applicable Document: Terms & Conditions, Age Gate, Registration Form
Keywords for Detection: age restriction, 18 years, under 18, age of majority, parental consent required, children, minor, age declaration, not for children
Evidence Required: T&C age clause, Age gate implementation, Age declaration checkbox records
Compliance Test Question: Do the Terms & Conditions include age restrictions and requirements for parental consent for users below 18?
Sample Compliant Language: By using our services, you represent and warrant that you are at least 18 years of age, or that you are between 13 and 18 years of age and have obtained verifiable consent from a parent or lawful guardian to use our services. If you are under 18, you may use our services only under the supervision of a parent or guardian. We do not knowingly collect personal data from children under 18 without parental consent. If we learn that we have collected data from a child without valid consent, we will delete it promptly.
Remediation Recommendation: Add an age declaration clause in T&C. Implement an age gate. Create a separate children's consent workflow with parental verification.
Notes: The age declaration in T&C supplements but does not replace the need for verifiable parental consent under Section 6(1).

DPDP Section Title: Data Principal Duties - Accuracy of Information
Requirement Description: Data Principals must not provide false or misleading personal data and must ensure the data they provide is accurate
Plain English Explanation: Users are responsible for making sure the information they give you is correct and not fake.
Applicable Document: Terms & Conditions, Privacy Policy, Registration Form
Keywords for Detection: accurate information, correct data, false information, misleading data, you warrant, you represent, accuracy obligation, truthful information
Evidence Required: T&C accuracy clause, User declaration checkbox, Account update mechanism
Compliance Test Question: Do the Terms & Conditions require Data Principals to provide accurate and truthful personal data?
Sample Compliant Language: You are responsible for ensuring that the personal data you provide to us is accurate, complete, and up-to-date. You warrant that all information provided during registration and throughout your use of our services is true and correct. You agree to promptly update your personal information if any changes occur.
Remediation Recommendation: Add a user accuracy obligation clause in T&C. Provide easy self-service update functionality. Send periodic data accuracy reminders.
Notes: This is a user obligation, not a Data Fiduciary obligation. However, the Data Fiduciary must still verify accuracy for high-stakes decisions.

DPDP Section Title: Data Principal Duties - Compliance with Law
Requirement Description: Data Principals must comply with the provisions of the DPDP Act and not impersonate another person while providing personal data
Plain English Explanation: Users must follow the law and cannot pretend to be someone else when giving you their information.
Applicable Document: Terms & Conditions, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: comply with law, impersonate, not impersonate, false identity, another person, misrepresentation, identity fraud, compliance with DPDP
Evidence Required: T&C compliance clause, Identity verification mechanism, Fraud detection logs
Compliance Test Question: Do the Terms & Conditions prohibit impersonation and require compliance with the DPDP Act?
Sample Compliant Language: You agree to comply with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 and all applicable data protection laws. You must not provide personal data that impersonates another person or entity. You must not use false identities or provide information about another person without their authorization.
Remediation Recommendation: Add a compliance and non-impersonation clause. Implement identity verification for high-risk transactions. Monitor for fraudulent account creation.
Notes: Primarily a deterrent clause. Actual enforcement relies on identity verification and fraud detection systems.

DPDP Section Title: Consequences of False Information or Misrepresentation
Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary may terminate services or restrict access if Data Principal provides false information or violates DPDP obligations
Plain English Explanation: You can suspend or terminate a user's account if they give you false information or break their obligations under the data protection law.
Applicable Document: Terms & Conditions, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: terminate, suspend, restrict access, false information consequence, violation consequence, account termination, service restriction, breach of obligations
Evidence Required: T&C consequence clause, Termination procedure, Historical termination records
Compliance Test Question: Do the Terms & Conditions specify consequences for providing false information or violating user obligations under the DPDP Act?
Sample Compliant Language: If you provide false, inaccurate, or misleading personal data, or if you violate your obligations under the DPDP Act, we reserve the right to suspend or terminate your account and restrict access to our services. We will notify you of such action unless prohibited by law.
Remediation Recommendation: Include a graduated enforcement clause (warning → suspension → termination). Ensure the clause is fair and proportionate.
Notes: Exercise caution in enforcing termination. Disproportionate enforcement may lead to regulatory complaints.

Requirement Description: Personal data must be encrypted both in transit and at rest as part of reasonable security safeguards
Plain English Explanation: You must encrypt user data when sending it over the internet (in transit) and when storing it (at rest) to protect it from unauthorized access.
Applicable Document: Information Security Policy, Privacy Policy, Technical Architecture Document
Keywords for Detection: encryption, encrypted, TLS, SSL, AES, in transit, at rest, end-to-end encryption, secure transmission, cipher
Evidence Required: Encryption configuration records, SSL/TLS certificates, Encryption at rest configuration, Penetration test reports
Compliance Test Question: Is personal data encrypted during transmission and while stored?
Sample Compliant Language: We protect your personal data using industry-standard encryption: (a) Data in transit is protected using TLS 1.2 or higher; (b) Data at rest is encrypted using AES-256 encryption; and (c) Passwords are hashed using [algorithm, e.g., bcrypt]. Our encryption practices are regularly reviewed and updated to maintain security.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement TLS 1.2+ for all data transmission. Enable AES-256 encryption for databases and storage. Use strong password hashing. Conduct regular encryption audits.
Notes: Encryption is a baseline security requirement. Absence of encryption is viewed as a serious deficiency by regulators.

Requirement Description: Personal data access must be restricted to authorized personnel on a need-to-know basis through role-based access controls
Plain English Explanation: Only employees who need to access user data for their job should be able to access it. Use role-based permissions.
Applicable Document: Access Control Policy, Information Security Policy, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: access control, role-based, RBAC, need to know, authorized personnel, restricted access, permission, authorization, least privilege, access matrix
Evidence Required: Access control matrix, Role definitions, Access audit logs, Privileged access management records
Compliance Test Question: Are access controls implemented to ensure personal data is accessible only to authorized personnel on a need-to-know basis?
Sample Compliant Language: Access to personal data is strictly limited to authorized employees who require it to perform their duties. We implement role-based access controls (RBAC) ensuring employees can access only the data necessary for their role. All access is logged and regularly audited.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement RBAC. Create role-based access matrices. Enable comprehensive access logging. Conduct quarterly access reviews. Remove access upon role change or termination.
Notes: Access reviews are critical. Orphaned accounts and excessive permissions are common audit findings.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must have mechanisms to detect, respond to, and recover from security incidents affecting personal data
Plain English Explanation: You must have systems to detect security problems (like hacks), respond to them quickly, and recover from them.
Applicable Document: Incident Response Plan, Security Operations Procedures, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: incident response, security incident, breach detection, detect, respond, recover, security operations, SOC, monitoring, alerting, incident management
Evidence Required: Incident response plan, Detection tool configurations, Response playbooks, Post-incident review records
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization have documented security incident detection, response, and recovery procedures?
Sample Compliant Language: We maintain security monitoring systems to detect unauthorized access or anomalies in personal data processing. Our incident response team is available [24/7] to investigate and respond to security incidents. In the event of a personal data breach, we follow our Incident Response Plan and notify affected parties and the Data Protection Board as required by law.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement SIEM or security monitoring tools. Create incident response playbooks. Conduct tabletop exercises. Establish a security incident response team.
Notes: Detection capability is as important as response. Many breaches go undetected for months.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary must provide regular training to employees who handle personal data on data protection obligations and security practices
Plain English Explanation: You must train your employees who handle user data about data protection rules and security best practices.
Applicable Document: Training Policy, Privacy Policy, Employee Handbook
Keywords for Detection: training, employee training, staff training, awareness, data protection training, security training, privacy training, education, personnel training
Evidence Required: Training curriculum, Training attendance records, Training completion certificates, Training effectiveness assessments
Compliance Test Question: Does the organization provide regular data protection and security training to employees who handle personal data?
Sample Compliant Language: All employees who handle personal data undergo mandatory data protection training upon joining and refresher training [annually]. Our training covers: DPDP Act obligations, data security best practices, incident reporting, and handling of Data Principal rights requests. Training completion is tracked and documented.
Remediation Recommendation: Develop a DPDP-focused training curriculum. Mandate training for all employees handling personal data. Track completion. Conduct refresher training annually.
Notes: Training records are evidence of compliance. Document who was trained, when, and on what topics.

DPDP Section Title: Cookie Consent and Tracking Technology Compliance
Requirement Description: Use of cookies and tracking technologies that collect personal data requires prior consent with clear information about the tracking purpose
Plain English Explanation: Before placing cookies or trackers that collect personal data, you must get consent and explain what the tracking is for.
Applicable Document: Cookie Policy, Cookie Banner, Privacy Policy, Consent Management Platform
Keywords for Detection: cookie, cookies, tracking, pixel, beacon, analytics, third-party cookie, cookie consent, cookie banner, cookie policy, tracking technology, web beacon, fingerprinting
Evidence Required: Cookie banner configuration, Cookie inventory, Consent records for cookies, CMP configuration
Compliance Test Question: Does the website/app obtain consent before placing non-essential cookies or tracking technologies that collect personal data?
Sample Compliant Language: We use cookies and similar tracking technologies to [describe purposes: e.g., operate the website, analyze traffic, personalize content]. Before placing any non-essential cookies that collect personal data, we obtain your consent through our cookie banner. You can manage your cookie preferences at any time by clicking [Cookie Settings]. Essential cookies necessary for website operation are placed based on our legitimate interest.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement a consent-managed cookie banner. Classify cookies as essential/non-essential. Block non-essential cookies until consent is obtained. Provide granular cookie preferences.
Notes: Cookie consent under DPDP follows the same standards as general consent: free, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous.

DPDP Section Title: Prohibition on Cookie Tracking for Children
Requirement Description: Tracking cookies and behavioral monitoring cookies must not be placed on devices of users known to be children under 18
Plain English Explanation: If you know a user is under 18, you cannot put tracking cookies on their device or monitor their online behavior through cookies.
Applicable Document: Cookie Policy, Children's Privacy Policy, Technical Configuration
Keywords for Detection: no tracking children, children cookies, minor cookie tracking, child tracking prohibition, disable cookies children, behavioral cookies children
Evidence Required: Technical configuration blocking tracking cookies for minors, Cookie policy with children's provision, Age-based cookie restriction logs
Compliance Test Question: Are tracking and behavioral monitoring cookies disabled for users identified as children under 18?
Sample Compliant Language: We do not place tracking cookies, behavioral monitoring cookies, or advertising cookies on devices of users identified as being under 18 years of age. When our age verification mechanism identifies a minor user, all non-essential cookies are automatically blocked, and only strictly necessary operational cookies are permitted.
Remediation Recommendation: Implement age-based cookie restrictions. Flag minor accounts. Configure CMP to suppress tracking cookies for flagged accounts. Audit cookie placement for child users.
Notes: Technical enforcement is essential. Policy statements alone are insufficient for this absolute prohibition.

DPDP Section Title: SDF Obligation - Resident DPO in India
Requirement Description: Significant Data Fiduciaries must appoint a Data Protection Officer who is resident in India
Plain English Explanation: If you're a Significant Data Fiduciary, your DPO must live in India - not remotely from another country.
Applicable Document: DPO Appointment, Privacy Policy
Keywords for Detection: resident in India, India-based, based in India, DPO resident, local DPO, Indian DPO, physically present in India
Evidence Required: DPO appointment letter, DPO address proof, Residency documentation, Contact details
Compliance Test Question: If an SDF, is the appointed Data Protection Officer resident in India with verifiable contact details?
Sample Compliant Language: [If applicable: Our Data Protection Officer is resident in India and can be contacted at: [Name], [Address in India], [Email], [Phone]. The DPO is available during Indian business hours.]
Remediation Recommendation: If SDF, appoint a DPO physically resident in India. Maintain Indian address and contact details. Ensure availability during Indian business hours.
Notes: The residency requirement is strict. A DPO based in Singapore or the US does not comply.

DPDP Section Title: SDF Obligation - Business Continuity Plan for Data Protection
Requirement Description: Significant Data Fiduciaries must have a business continuity plan ensuring continuity of data protection during disruptions
Plain English Explanation: If you're a Significant Data Fiduciary, you need a plan to keep protecting data even during emergencies like natural disasters or cyberattacks.
Applicable Document: Business Continuity Plan, Disaster Recovery Plan
Keywords for Detection: business continuity, BCP, disaster recovery, DR plan, continuity plan, resilience, operational continuity, emergency plan
Evidence Required: Business Continuity Plan, DR plan, Testing records, Plan review documentation
Compliance Test Question: If an SDF, does the organization have a business continuity plan that addresses data protection continuity?
Sample Compliant Language: [Internal document] As a Significant Data Fiduciary, we maintain a Business Continuity Plan that ensures the continuity of personal data protection during disruptions. The plan covers: data backup procedures, alternative processing arrangements, emergency contact protocols, and post-disruption recovery procedures.
Remediation Recommendation: If SDF, develop a BCP with specific data protection continuity measures. Test the plan annually. Update based on test results.
Notes: This is an SDF-specific obligation that goes beyond general security requirements.

DPDP Section Title: Grievance Officer Must Be Distinct from Regular Customer Support
Requirement Description: The Grievance Officer for data protection matters should be distinct from general customer support, with specific authority and training on DPDP matters
Plain English Explanation: Your Grievance Officer for data protection should be a specific person with proper training, not just your regular customer service team.
Applicable Document: Grievance Redressal Policy, Organization Chart
Keywords for Detection: dedicated grievance officer, distinct from support, not customer service, specific authority, DPDP-trained, data protection expertise, designated officer
Evidence Required: Org chart showing Grievance Officer role, Appointment letter specifying DPDP authority, Training records
Compliance Test Question: Is the Grievance Officer a distinct role with specific authority and training on DPDP matters, separate from general customer support?
Sample Compliant Language: [Internal document] Our Grievance Officer is a designated role with specific authority to address DPDP-related complaints. The Grievance Officer is distinct from our general customer support function and has received specialized training on the DPDP Act. The Grievance Officer reports directly to [senior management/DPO/Board] and has the authority to escalate matters and recommend corrective actions.
Remediation Recommendation: Designate a specific Grievance Officer with clear DPDP authority. Provide DPDP-specific training. Ensure independence from general customer service metrics.
Notes: While the Act does not explicitly require separation from customer support, a dedicated officer demonstrates seriousness and ensures expertise.

Requirement Description: Data Principal has the right to nominate any individual who shall exercise their rights in case of death or incapacity
Plain English Explanation: Users can appoint someone (like a family member) to handle their data rights if they die or become unable to do so themselves.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Nomination Form, Account Settings
Keywords for Detection: nominate, nomination, representative, exercise rights on behalf, death, incapacity, personal representative, nominee, authorized person
Evidence Required: Nomination mechanism, Nomination form, Process for verifying nominee authority
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy inform Data Principals of their right to nominate a representative and provide a mechanism to do so?
Sample Compliant Language: You have the right to nominate any individual who will exercise your rights under this Privacy Policy in the event of your death or incapacity. To nominate a representative, please complete the nomination form available at [link] or contact our Grievance Officer. You may change or revoke your nomination at any time.
Remediation Recommendation: Create a nomination mechanism (form in account settings or downloadable form). Establish verification procedures for nominees. Maintain nomination records securely.
Notes: This is a unique DPDP feature not found in GDPR. It addresses succession planning for digital assets.

Requirement Description: Data Principal has the right to have readily available means of registering a grievance with the Data Fiduciary
Plain English Explanation: Users have the right to easily file complaints about how you handle their data.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Grievance Form, Terms & Conditions
Keywords for Detection: right to grievance, grievance redressal, complaint, register grievance, raise complaint, grievance mechanism, redressal right
Evidence Required: Grievance mechanism, Grievance form, Accessibility of grievance channel, Grievance handling records
Compliance Test Question: Does the Privacy Policy clearly inform Data Principals of their right to grievance redressal and provide accessible means to register a grievance?
Sample Compliant Language: You have the right to grievance redressal regarding our processing of your personal data. You may register a grievance by: (1) Completing the grievance form at [link]; (2) Emailing [grievance officer email]; or (3) Calling [phone number]. We will acknowledge your grievance within [2 days] and respond within [30 days].
Remediation Recommendation: Provide multiple accessible channels for grievance registration. Ensure the process is simple and well-publicized. Track and respond within timelines.
Notes: This is a standalone right under Section 9(1)(d), separate from the general grievance mechanism obligation under Section 10.

Requirement Description: Data Fiduciary may charge a reasonable fee for manifestly unfounded or excessive requests, but basic rights exercise must be free
Plain English Explanation: You cannot charge users for exercising their data rights in normal circumstances. You can only charge a fee if someone is clearly abusing the system with unreasonable repeated requests.
Applicable Document: Privacy Policy, Fee Schedule, Rights Request Procedure
Keywords for Detection: no fee, free of charge, reasonable fee, manifestly unfounded, excessive request, charge for request, fee for access, cost of request
Evidence Required: Fee policy, Decision records for fee imposition, Fee waiver documentation
Compliance Test Question: Is the exercise of data principal rights free of charge, with fees imposed only for manifestly unfounded or excessive requests?
Sample Compliant Language: We do not charge any fee for exercising your data protection rights. However, we reserve the right to charge a reasonable fee or refuse to act on manifestly unfounded or excessive requests (for example, repetitive requests). Where we charge a fee, we will inform you of the amount and basis before proceeding.
Remediation Recommendation: Do not charge fees for standard rights requests. Establish criteria for determining 'manifestly unfounded or excessive' requests. Document any fee decisions.
Notes: Fee imposition is the exception, not the rule. Most rights requests should be handled free of charge.
